main_summary.summary: Cal Luper, son of Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council adviser Clara Luper, was interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program (University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill) on May 24, 2011, at the Luper household in Oklahoma City for the Civil Rights History Project. Cal recalls the 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-ins in downtown Oklahoma City, the trip with his sister Marilyn Luper to New York City for the play Brother President, and the family living room where the Youth Council met and where a recovered sofa survived as a relic of the sit-in movement. He describes the segregated downtown landscape (the Skirvin and Huckins hotels, Bishop's Restaurant, Johnny Browns), his grandmother's daily two-bus commute from the Carverdale neighborhood, and how Clara Luper insisted Youth Council members carry schoolbooks during sit-ins. Cal names key allies of his mother in Oklahoma City: Dr. Charles N. Atkins, his wife and future state representative Hannah Atkins, Reverend Bratzinger of Fifth Street Baptist, Reverend Alexander of the AME church at 13th and Kelham, state senator and attorney Melvin B. Porter, and YMCA director Sam Cornelius. He reflects on weighing Black Power militance after military service against the nonviolent discipline he learned in the Youth Council.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Cal Luper is a first-person witness to the 1958 Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-ins, often credited as one of the earliest organized sit-in campaigns of the modern civil rights movement, predating Greensboro by more than a year. His testimony adds the household-level texture of the campaign that is absent from the public record: the Luper living-room sofa burned by a Molotov cocktail, the Commanders security unit of the Youth Council, the practice of carrying schoolbooks to lunch counters, and the named network of OKC Black ministers, physicians, attorneys, and educators who sustained Clara Luper's organizing.

The interview also documents the cross-generational reach of the Oklahoma City movement into the next decade — Melvin B. Porter's path from Vanderbilt Law to the Oklahoma State Senate, Hannah Atkins's election as state representative, and Cal's own reflection on whether to join Black Power organizing after Vietnam-era military service. Together these details make the interview a primary source for the local infrastructure of an under-documented chapter in sit-in history.

chapters.title: Interview Opening
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the recording at the Luper household in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, with filmmaker John Bishop, framing the interview within the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Cal Luper greets Mosnier and references storm weather outside.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project interview series

chapters.title: Brother President and the Youth Council
chapters.summary: Cal recalls his sister Marilyn Luper and the 1958 NAACP Youth Council trip to New York City with the play Brother President. He describes a surviving film of the performance held at a local OKC photo shop and introduces other family members involved in the Youth Council, including his cousins Betty Germany and Richard Brown, an original sit-in participant.

chapters.title: The Sit-In Sofa
chapters.summary: Cal describes the Luper family living-room sofa where Youth Council meetings were held — recovered in the 1980s after surviving the sit-in movement, including scorching from a Molotov cocktail thrown through the front door of the house. He frames the recovered sofa as a physical relic of the campaign.

chapters.title: Downtown OKC Segregation
chapters.summary: Cal describes the segregated geography of downtown Oklahoma City — the Skirvin and Huckins hotels were closed to Black patrons except as workers, and most lunch counters refused service. He recalls the Hudson hotel elevators and the contrast of being able to walk a New York lobby without being told to leave. The Katz Drug Store stood at Main and Robinson where the Ford Theater now stands.

chapters.title: Grandmother's Commute and Carverdale
chapters.summary: Cal describes his grandmother's daily two-bus commute from the Carverdale neighborhood — a predominantly Black addition near Northeast Tenth and what is now Martin Luther King Avenue — to downtown Oklahoma City, with transfers near Johnny Browns. He gives her address as 1236 Windermere and notes she was the family's willing babysitter.

chapters.title: Youth Council Discipline and the Commanders
chapters.summary: Cal describes the self-discipline structure of the Oklahoma City Youth Council, including a unit called the Commanders responsible for protecting members from outside harassment and from internal misconduct. Clara Luper required participants to carry schoolbooks to sit-ins at Katz Drug Store and Bishop's Restaurant, where Cal studied first-year Spanish while seated at the counter. Bishop's locked its doors against demonstrators.

chapters.title: Clara Luper's Public Bearing and Radio Show
chapters.summary: Cal describes how his mother conducted herself when negotiating with restaurant owners, mayors, governors, and police chiefs — looking directly at them rather than performing the deferential posture expected of Black Oklahomans. He recalls working with her on the Clara Luper radio show on Southeast 44th and Bryant for over thirty years, and the comedic call-and-response format they developed.

chapters.title: Cal's Education and Electrical Trade
chapters.summary: Cal describes his time at the University of Oklahoma as a history major and how his attention was divided between school and his mother's organizing. He left to attend trade school for electrical work, earned a license, and worked alongside his father — also an electrician — for a couple of years before his father's death.

chapters.title: Nonviolence Versus Black Power
chapters.summary: Cal reflects on returning from military service with weapons proficiency and considering Black Power organizing as a path forward. He argues against the strategic logic of armed confrontation — that nonviolence reaches a person's heart while a fist cannot beat a gun — and observes that even nonviolent activists studied judo because there is always somebody tougher. He concludes he would rather concede a point than be beaten to prove one.

chapters.title: Family Cost and Morning Discipline
chapters.summary: Cal reflects on whether the family paid too high a price for his mother's organizing, recalling how the household's morning rhythm — meet at the church by nine, walk downtown to the sit-in target — set a lifelong pattern. He describes meeting at Calvary Baptist under Reverend Kerry (Morris Kerry), close enough to walk to Katz Drug Store, Bishop's Restaurant, or Johnny Browns.

chapters.title: Allies of Clara Luper
chapters.summary: Cal names key allies of his mother: Reverend Bratzinger of Fifth Street Baptist; Reverend Alexander of the AME church at 13th and Kelham; Dr. Charles N. Atkins, a Bahamian-born physician who debated strategy with Clara, brought babies into the world, and treated arthritis on the East Side; Hannah Atkins, his wife, later an Oklahoma state representative; and their son Ed, Cal's close friend.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

chapters.title: Melvin B. Porter and Sam Cornelius
chapters.summary: Cal describes meeting Melvin B. Porter on the return trip from the Brother President performance in New York — Porter, then at Vanderbilt Law School, boarded the bus when he saw the Oklahoma banner and committed to opening a law firm in Oklahoma City. Porter became an Oklahoma state senator, and his sons Melvin Jr. and Joel continue the firm on Northwest 23rd and Hudson. Cal also recalls Sam Cornelius, director of the East Side YMCA where Monday-night Youth Council meetings were held before the Freedom Center opened, and Cornelius's children Bruce and Charlene (Cal's Douglass High School classmate).

chapters.title: The Board of Education and Closing
chapters.summary: Cal explains the family joke of the Board of Education — a wooden board carved by Alexander Jones, mechanical arts teacher at Dunjee School and Clara Luper's commute partner from the 1500 block of NE Park Place to the 1800 block where Clara lived. Cal closes by referencing Professor McClure of the OU desegregation effort. The interview ends with the standard LoC/NMAAHC sign-off.
